data.title: Pitney Bowes Moves From Hold To Buy On Stronger Fundamentals (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Q4 numbers for Pitney Bowes Inc. (PBI) finally broke the stock’s slide. The stock jumped about 9% after the open following yesterday's results, and that was enough for me to rethink my Hold rating. I’d stuck with Hold because sales kept dropping, the old core business kept shrinking, and there was still over $2 billion in debt on the balance sheet. That call made sense at the time; the company hadn’t found a way to stop losing sales for good, and the stock just kept drifting down, from $11.16 to $9.48 last quarter. Sales were flat, the debt was heavy, and most people figured the buybacks and dividends just weren’t enough to draw in new long-term investors.

This quarter doesn’t mean the big-picture decline in mail volume is over. Instead, what stands out is that management finally got serious about cutting costs and being picky about where the money goes. That shift paid off. Now the company’s actually generating solid cash, and the debt is coming down, even without any sales growth.

Looking at the basics: Q4 adjusted diluted EPS hit $0.45, which beat estimates by $0.07. That wasn’t because sales picked up; in fact, Q4 sales landed at $477.6 million, just under $5 million short of what analysts thought and down 7% from a year ago. The EPS beat came from running a tighter ship, not from new business coming in.

But cash flow told a different story. Cash from operations for the year closed at $383 million, up 39% compared to last year, while free cash flow was $358 million, up 24%. Earlier quarters had been stuck in place, so this was a clear turnaround, mainly thanks to better margins and smarter handling of working capital. Adjusted EBIT for the year reached $461 million, a 20% jump from $385 million last year, and total Segment EBITDA was $660 million, up from $632 million. Net debt to adjusted EBITDA is now just under 3 times, which is a big step forward for the balance sheet. The risk on the stock is lower now, and the company’s in better shape for whatever changes come next.

SendTech’s Q4 EBIT rose 24%. They cut costs and tweaked their lineup. Presort EBIT fell 20%. Management had tried pushing prices higher before, but that just made important customers leave. Now they’re changing course. Management acknowledged that prior pricing decisions hurt volumes and indicated that beginning in 2026 they will prioritize regaining profitable volume, even if it pressures near-term margins. Presort’s new pitch to customers should help get volume back up before too long, but in the meantime, EBIT will probably keep getting squeezed.

Wall Street expects revenue to stay about flat into 2026 and 2027. The consensus has sales at roughly $1.85 billion in each of those years and EPS ticking up from $1.43 to $1.6. Not a big leap, but at least earnings are set to grow a bit, and margins are much better. The stock trades at about 7 times forward earnings. The market clearly doubts how long this can last and worries about whether they’ll have to keep reinvesting. If they can just keep sales steady and keep free cash flow around $300 million, that alone could justify the stock trading at a higher multiple, since the business brings in real cash. Management’s giving money back, too; they approved major buybacks through 2025 and increased the dividend from $0.05 to $0.09 per share during 2025, roughly an 80% cumulative increase. That’s not something you usually see from a company whose sales aren’t growing.

Risks are obvious here. Regular mail keeps shrinking, so there’s just not much room for this business to grow. Cutting costs will only get them so far, especially if the customer base keeps shrinking. They’re still at risk if the plan to stabilize mail volumes in Presort stalls out, or if SendTech loses more clients than they can keep, or if they just can’t squeeze any more savings out of the business, which seems to be happening already, since they’ve gotten less out of restructuring this year than before. The strategic review could matter a lot, but it’s a coin flip: if they can pull off something like a Presort joint venture or a sale, the stock could go up. If they mess it up or make a deal that’s confusing or dilutes value, it could go the other way. Debt isn’t as scary as it was, but it’s still a lot, and with all those convertible notes, there could be more dilution if the stock stops going up. If things get worse, like if revenue drops faster or the economy turns and free cash flow falls under $250 million, the valuation could drop to 5x earnings or less, and the stock might sink to $8 as people worry about risk and liquidity.

On the other hand, if Presort starts landing new customers, SendTech stops losing accounts, and free cash flow gets back over $350 million while debt keeps coming down toward 2x, then the stock could get an 8-9x earnings multiple. That would put the share price around $13-$14, especially if investors start believing management can keep returning capital.

After a bunch of revenue and profit misses in 2025, sentiment hit bottom, but now it’s turning. The stock’s bounced after Q4, and people seem to be noticing that management’s actually delivering better results than expected, but no one’s really pricing in a full turnaround yet. So, the risk for new investors isn’t huge, but the upside if things stabilize looks underappreciated. Plus, you’re still getting a good yield.

I’m rating Pitney Bowes a Buy. It’s not risk-free; I know there’s no growth in the industry, and management still has a lot to prove, but they’re generating reliable free cash flow, paying down debt faster, and focusing on returning capital to shareholders. That’s more important now than worrying about the stock staying cheap forever. I wouldn’t call it a Hold unless free cash flow drops below $300 million, or if they go back to cutting prices in Presort just to keep volume, or if net debt rises back above 3.5x adjusted EBITDA. None of that’s happening right now. If cash flow weakens, or if management can’t win back customers in the next few quarters, I’ll rethink. But for now, with strict cost control and decent results even without sales growth, I see more room for the stock to go up.

data.title: Macro Insights: Death Of A Software Salesman, The Great Rotation, And Dot-Com 2.0
data.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the SA’s The Macro Brief! This official SA Profile will highlight our analysts' latest economic and market analysis to help investors gauge the ever-volatile financial landscape through various recurring series.

The economy is the foundation of financial markets, influencing everything from corporate earnings and consumer spending to central bank policy and inflation. Understanding economic trends, policy decisions, and sector activity can be critical when assessing market opportunities and making informed investment decisions.

Check out these must-reads from February’s first half…

Trending Themes

Fear Is Growing

Buyer Beware: The Market's AI Bubble Risk Just Got Even Bigger | Samuel Smith | 2/7/26

“A massive spending surge is quietly reshaping the market’s risk profile. What looks like innovation may be creating a dangerous new dependency. One overlooked corner of the market could benefit enormously.”

Sell The "Dead Cat" Bounce, The Bear Market Risks Are Rising | Damir Tokic | 2/10/26

“Hedge funds are making massive short bets against tech, and the recent bounce was likely due to short covering. Thus, the selloff is likely to continue as the AI bubble continues to burst. In addition, the cyclical rotation trade is likely to falter, given the recent negative economic data.”

3 Warning Signs The Stock Market Is Overdue For A Sharp Correction | High Yield Investor | 2/11/26

“While the S&P 500 (SPY) and NASDAQ (QQQ) have been on tremendous bull runs since 2023, there are three historically reliable warning signs emerging simultaneously that indicate that the market could be due for a significant correction in the near future.”

AI Capex Boom Vs. The Dot-Com Bubble: The Striking Similarities And Three Big Concerns | Hawkinvest | 2/11/26

“Many major market indexes just hit new record highs, and earnings were strong this quarter. But, underneath all of this bullishness, there are some major concerns. This is especially true when you look at the massive capex spending plans the hyperscalers updated us with when they announced their earnings reports.”

The 'Smart Money' Isn't Buying This Market | Bret Jensen | 2/14/26

“Insiders and retail investors are currently providing divergent signals about the market's direction, which should signal caution on equities. Corporate insiders and directors are selling stocks, and Warren Buffett built up a record cash hoard before exiting leadership at Berkshire Hathaway after 60 years at the helm.”

An $80 Billion Liquidity Storm May Be About To Hit Stocks This Week | Mott Capital Management | 2/15/26

“Persistent Treasury settlements are draining liquidity, pressuring the stock market with more frequent and deeper declines on settlement days. Excess liquidity from the Fed's Reverse Repo facility is exhausted, forcing new Treasury funding to come from risk asset sales. Market weakness is likely to persist until at least April, when Treasury issuance may slow post-tax season, potentially easing pressure temporarily.”

‘SaaS-pocalypse’

The Magnificent Seven Maintains Its Winning Ways Even As Software Shows Cracks | JR Research | 2/2/26

“This couldn't have been a more bifurcated times for the tech sector leaders (XLK). As the market rotation themes got underway, tech has seen an underlying disconnect in the AI value chain, hitting the software providers hard.”

I'm Buying The Software Meltdown | Jack Bowman | 2/4/26

“Software stocks have been sharply oversold on AI disruption fears, creating compelling value opportunities for patient investors. The narrative that AI will render established software firms obsolete is overstated; domain expertise and human capital remain critical. There is no substitute for expertise, despite AI efficiency gains.”

AI Bubble, Tech Funeral? Who Will Fail And Who Will Double Down? | Agar Capital | 2/12/26

“AI-driven disruption is triggering a sharp selloff in data, consulting, and SaaS companies, exposing structural vulnerabilities in their high-fee, recurring revenue models…The market now sharply distinguishes between firms monetizing AI and those burning cash without clear profitability, pressuring SaaS and service-based stocks’ multiples.”

Software Is Finally Cracking - And The Great Rotation Is Picking Up Speed | Leo Nelissen | 2/15/26

“AI disruption is accelerating market rotation, weakening the competitive edge of many software companies, and favoring value-oriented sectors like industrials, energy, and midstream. I focus on companies with durable business models, wide moats, and resistance to AI disruption.”

Conclusion

We hope our analysts' insights serve as a valuable financial compass for investors seeking to make informed investment decisions.

Thanks for reading. Tell us what you think by commenting below! If you'd like to see more, follow this account.

Please let us know if you have any suggestions you’d like to see added to this series and any ideas for new additional recurring series. We're always open to your valuable feedback.

To stay current on the latest economic and market events coming up, subscribe to Wall Street Breakfast, Seeking Alpha's flagship daily business news summary.

Disclaimer: Written by SA Editors

data.title: Hinge Health: More Immune To AI Than Most; A Fast-Growing, Highly Cash-Flowing Market Leader
data.description:
Executive Summary

Hinge Health (HNGE) is a leading digital health platform addressing musculoskeletal (MSK) conditions. It uses computer vision-guided exercise therapy, virtual and AI-powered coaching, connected wearables, and on-demand access to physical therapists and orthopedic specialists to provide care to people with all manner of MSK conditions (e.g., back, neck, shoulder, knee). It contracts with employers (primarily large enterprises), currently serving over 2,800 of them and covering 24.6 million lives as of the end of 2025. Founded in 2014 and still led by its co-founders Daniel Perez (CEO) and Gabriel Mecklenburg (Executive Chairman), Hinge went public in May 2025 and, since going public, has continued to demonstrate operational excellence.

After being swept up in the “AI SaaSpocalypse” selloff that left the stock down 32% YTD, Hinge reported strong (verging on stellar) 4Q25 results that sent the stock up 17% last Wednesday, though the stock is still down -14% YTD. I have been aware of Hinge since the IPO but lost track of it through the balance of 2025. I began looking back into it leading up to earnings and, after the most recent report, feel the business is executing exceptionally, has a strong growth story still left ahead of it, is defensible against AI headwinds, and is currently priced quite cheaply.

Some of the key things that make Hinge attractive:

  • Growth: Revenue grew 46% in 4Q25, bringing full 2025 revenue growth to 51%. For 2026, management guided to 39% growth in 1Q26 and 25% for full 2026 (a number that feels very beatable, similar to 2025’s guidance). Revenue growth was driven by a 23% increase in covered lives and an increase in yield (covered lives that actually use Hinge) from 3.4% to 3.9%.

  • Profitability: Notwithstanding stock-based compensation (SBC) related to the May IPO (which will continue to be a bit of a GAAP drag over the next 4-8 quarters), Hinge is actively expanding (non-GAAP) gross margins (up 5 pts to 83% in 2025) and has been driving substantial free cash flow expansion, with FCF margin coming in at 31% for 2025 (nearly triple 2024’s 12%). Non-GAAP operating margin also flipped substantially positive to 20% in 2025—it is safe to say Hinge experienced a major operating leverage inflection in 2025. For its size/growth, Hinge’s profitability is unrivaled in the digital health tech space.

  • Market Opportunity: The US physical therapy market represents $60-70 billion annually (~100x Hinge’s current annual revenue), with 40% of US adults experiencing an MSK condition and 9% seeking physical therapy annually. Hinge believes it can continue growth both by continuing to add new covered lives (only 25 million as of the end of 2025) and by increasing yield (engagement) from the current 3.9% towards the 9% figure.

  • Valuation: At the current ~$39/share price, Hinge is trading at 14.6x 2026 FCF (based on management’s 2026 guidance, which all but certainly will be beaten). In my Bull Case (discussed in Valuation below), which I think is closer to what the company will actually achieve in 2026 than management’s guidance, Hinge is currently trading at 12.6x 2026 FCF.

  • Cash Position and Buybacks: The company had nearly $500 million of cash and essentially no debt as of the end of 2025. Having already met their 30% long-term FCF margin target (set at IPO), management stated that they view 2026 as a year to incrementally invest back into the business (primarily R&D, new products/features, and incremental S&M). The cash position/generation also gives them dry powder for potential acquisitions, as well as the furtherance of their up to $250 million stock buyback plan, $65 million of which (1.4 million shares) had been executed as of the end of 2025.

More qualitative factors:

  • Market Leadership: From its 97% logo retention to its 110%+ net dollar retention to its 4.9 stars on the Apple App Store to customer testimonials to management’s commentary about frequently winning clients/customers from other providers, Hinge appears to be the true best-in-class market leader in digital physical therapy and MSK.

  • AI Defensibility: Due to its clear market leadership and the dynamics of the healthcare space (e.g., longer sales cycles, more relationship-based), I argue Hinge’s business and market position is more defensible than other software/SaaS companies (details below in SaaSpocalypse Defensibility). As the panic dies down and investors become more discriminating about AI’s impact on software in the coming quarters, I believe Hinge will eventually be acknowledged as much less vulnerable than other software companies in other verticals.

  • Growth Opportunities: Empowered by the cash flow generation of its core business, Hinge is free to invest in R&D and to be acquisitive (either competitively or expansionarily), further enforcing its moat.

  • Management: The founder-led management team inspires confidence when they speak, both in Hinge’s ability to remain differentiated/market-leading and in how large of an opportunity they see still ahead of them. The message of “we want to transform our segment of the healthcare system” actually lands (for me at least).

As it currently stands, Hinge presents essentially all of the things that an investor would want in a fundamentally sound business, and with strong medium-term growth tailwinds that should outweigh the secular and competitive risks.

On the risks side, the primary concern lately for all of software has been the potential disruption from AI, specifically a software development cost that is falling to zero, which enables the quick, cheap creation of new, potentially better-than-existing software products across all verticals. While I believe this is generally a valid concern (I believe many SaaS companies will indeed be disrupted or outright replaced, eventually), as I explain in the SaaSpocalypse Defensibility section below, I believe Hinge is more insulated from this risk than most software companies.

Other top risks to me include:

  • The potential emergence of a popular direct-to-consumer app available to all (not only those whose employers contract with Hinge or another provider) with similar AI- and computer vision-enabled offerings to Hinge. These already exist or have been tried to some degree, but there is always the risk of something really catching on, especially as better technology becomes more easily implemented.

  • Growth deceleration. It has begun to some degree (the 25% growth guidance for 2026 feels like sandbagging) but is inevitable over time. The key will be the pace of the deceleration over the next couple of years. While I believe Hinge can/will sustain 20%+ growth in 2027, 2028, and perhaps beyond (while also being a cash flow machine), I could be wrong.

The interesting thing about Hinge is that it is already being priced as if these risks are massive, imminent, and catastrophic, which I argue throughout this article is not at all the case.

I repeat: as of Friday’s closing price, Hinge is trading at 14.4x 2026 FCF (using conservative assumptions, which are likely to be exceeded), for a business that will still very likely be growing at ~20% in 2027 and beyond, has a best-in-class, clinically-validated platform, and has room to grow both in its core MSK product as well as via additional early-stage and future growth vectors. While there aren’t any great publicly-traded comps to benchmark against, and while investors may hold the fact that Hinge is a software/SaaSpocalypse name against it (for now at least), the current valuation is objectively very cheap if you believe it has medium- to long-term viability (which I do).

I think Hinge will continue to garner investor attention over the course of the year as it stacks substantially cash-flow-positive quarters, continues growing, and begins to paint the picture and execute on the plans for the future, and, while it may take a few quarters, I expect Hinge to “pass” the AI disruption test in investors’ minds and re-rate higher. A regaining of August 2025 highs would represent 40-50% upside.

Platform and Technology

Hinge aims to provide digital physical therapy care for MSK conditions (e.g., back, neck, shoulder, hip, knee, joint replacement therapy, post-surgery therapy, fall prevention, etc.) at a fraction of the cost of in-person physical therapy (since it takes place on an app without a personal therapist).

Hinge contracts with employers, primarily large enterprises (serving 45% of the Fortune 500), which then give employees (“members” or patients) the ability to enroll in Hinge by downloading the app. Once patients download the app, they complete intake questionnaires, after which they receive a treatment plan/regimen (generated by AI/ML) that they can begin immediately. The Hinge app uses computer vision to track patients while they do exercises, ensuring proper form. While the process is largely completely automated, patients also have on-demand access to a Care Team of physical therapists, physicians, and health coaches; Hinge has also integrated an always-on AI assistant called Robin, which helps members navigate the app and their treatment plan, as well as instantly provide patient support. Additionally, Hinge offers Enso, a wearable, non-invasive device (a TENS device) that has been shown to reduce pain and improve treatment outcomes; ~25% of members used Enso in 2025.

While I haven’t used it, the platform seems to be pretty darn good: Hinge is the 23rd most popular Medical app in the Apple App Store and, kind of remarkably, has a 4.9-star rating with 122k ratings.

Hinge has also conducted and published 21 peer-reviewed research studies (which you can browse here), including the following:

  • 73% reduction in knee replacements and 50% reduction in hip replacements (link)

  • 68% average reduction for back and joint pain in 10,000+ patient study (link)

  • 56% reduction in spinal fusion surgeries (link)

  • 60% reduction in X-rays and MRIs related to back pain (link)

  • 73% average pain reduction after acute injury (link)

You can imagine how these outcomes would be cost-saving for employers as patients avoid doctor’s visits, surgeries, etc. Hinge runs an annual analysis (in conjunction with Gallagher) to determine the cost savings that it delivers to clients/customers, with the most recent report finding a 2.4x ROI for fully-insured clients and an average of savings of $2,343 per participant (link).

Hinge’s 2,800 clients/customers include 53% of the Fortune 100 and 45% of the Fortune 500:

As a further example of Hinge’s impact for patients and employers that contract with them, you can read this case study from their website. It does a good job not only going over the benefits delivered to patients with MSK conditions but also describing exactly how Hinge partners with the employer and its cooperation with the administering healthcare company, in this case BlueCross BlueShield of Minnesota. The study of 2,277 patients/members at this anonymous employer reported:

From all accounts and appearances, the Hinge Health app is the best-in-class digital physical therapy platform that delivers both an enjoyable experience and good outcomes for patients.

Market Opportunity (TAM, etc.)

About 40% of US adults experience an MSK condition in a given year (WHO, 2021), resulting in approximately $381 billion of annual medical expenses. The MSK vertical is significant to health plans and self-insurers, ranking as a top three healthcare cost driver and a top five claims type (for manufacturing or labor-heavy companies, it can be the top cost driver).

The physical therapy market specifically represents $50-70 billion of annual spend, meaning Hinge is about 1% penetrated at its current scale.

Additionally, about 9% of US adults seek physical therapy for an MSK condition annually, a number that Hinge compares to its 3.9% covered lives yield as evidence of the significant room for further expansion within existing customers (management also thinks that eventually they can expand beyond 9%).

Additionally, Hinge estimates that something like three-quarters of patients referred to physical therapy never attend a visit, which speaks to the opportunity for a lower-friction, at-home, digital solution.

Within self-insured enterprises/organizations only, Hinge estimates a ~$10 billion opportunity, multiplying the 119 million self-insured lives by its 9% yield goal by ~$900/year ASP. While this is clearly aspirational, it shows that even reaching just 15-20% of that TAM represents a multiple-fold increase in revenue while also completely ignoring the opportunity in fully insured and federal insurance markets.

In the grand scheme of not only the physical therapy market but the broader MSK market, Hinge is still a very small fish. With its plans to increase both its market penetration and to expand the broader MSK market as a whole via its digital solution, Hinge should have ample room for growth ahead of it.

2025 Results

2025 results were pretty great, though there are some signs of (inevitable/potential) growth deceleration that are worth watching. Some 4Q25 highlights as outlined by the company:

Growth

In 2025, the company grew:

  • Clients from 2,256 to 2,830 (25%)

  • Covered lives from 20 million to 24.6 million (+23%)

  • Revenue from 51% to $588 million

It is important to note that revenue growth was driven by a combination of covered lives (+24%) and yield (3.9% versus 3.4%). The improvement in yield led to a 47% increase in the number of active members to 783k at year-end, which is really what drove the stellar revenue growth.

Yield has increased from 3.0% in 2023 to 3.4% in 2024 to 3.9% in 2025, and management uses the fact that 9% of the general population seeks physical therapy as a benchmark/goal for where their yield could be. Management attributes the yield expansion to product-led growth initiatives as well as targeted enrollment efforts (targeting members based on their prior claims history) and sounded confident that its yield trends will continue in 2026 on the earnings call, though their guidance assumes it to be flat.

Margins

Impressively, management stated that despite the 47% increase in active members, care team costs were flat in 2025, enabled by AI advancements that both lower human-requiring inquiries and allow each care team member to handle more inquiries.

Gross margins benefited, growing to 83% in 2025 (non-GAAP; GAAP gross margin was 80%), though this improvement was also attributable to efficiencies in hardware and infrastructure.

And then the real story: FCF margin nearly tripled to 31% for 2025. While GAAP metrics are made complicated by high IPO-related stock-based compensation, non-GAAP operating margin (which can basically be thought of as adjusted EBITDA margin) was also solid at 20% in 2025 (and 28% in 4Q25).

Other 2025 Metrics

  • Annual client retention rate of 97%

  • Net dollar retention “well above” 110%, the benchmark management views as indicative of a healthy business in its market.

In hindsight, this will probably be the most impressive inflection point/year for Hinge, though it really is not being appreciated as such by the market.

2026 Guidance

Revenue Guidance

Management guided to 39% revenue growth in 1Q26 and 25% in FY2026. This is baking in some pretty considerable deceleration over the course of 2026 but is very likely overly conservative. (Despite likely being conservative, it still came in about 5% higher than consensus estimates).

Importantly, CEO Perez detailed on the call that their 2026 guidance assumes flat yield in 2026. While this could certainly happen, when asked about it on the call, he was clearly confident that the company’s investments in product-led growth and targeted enrollment initiatives will continue to convert more active members.

The assumption of a flat yield in 2026 also implies that management believes active members will grow about 25% in 2026 (assuming stable ASP), which is essentially the same active member growth that was achieved in 2025 (a year that delivered 51% revenue growth…). Not saying another 50% growth year is going to happen, but there would appear to be significant upside to management’s revenue guidance.

Margins and Expenses

Management states that they plan to keep support team expenses flat again in 2026 (despite anticipated active member growth). Going forward, they expect any improvements in gross margin from AI automation and other efficiencies to largely be offset by increased penetration of the Enso hardware device.

For (non-GAAP) operating margin, management guides to a slight (1 pt) improvement to 21% in 2026. Management’s stated long-term operating margin goal is 25%. Management also states that they expect to be GAAP profitable in 2026.

While there was no guidance for FCF margin, we can expect a similar slight benefit from scale, operating leverage, and cost efficiencies, though it seems it will probably be partly or completely offset by R&D, S&M, and other investments management decides to make as it plans to invest back into the business.

Stock-Based Compensation

Stock-based compensation was $20-25 million in 4Q25, and CFO James Budge said on the call that that pace will continue for “probably at least the next 4 to 8 quarters.” The stock buyback program was initiated in part to combat this dilutive effect.

The company expects 85-87 million of fully diluted weighted average share count for 2026.

Investment in R&D, Headcount, Growth

Having already met its 30% FCF margin target set at IPO, management states that it regards 2026 as a year to invest incrementally back into the business, stating on the call that they plan to invest in R&D (growing R&D headcount) for new product initiatives (discussed in the next section), and sales and marketing to support the rollout and expansion of HingeSelect (discussed in the next section).

Other Product/Growth Drivers

Some near- and long-term growth drivers worth touching on:

Commercial Health Plans and Medicare

Currently, Hinge’s customer base is predominantly made up of large, self-insured employers—this cohort made up 22 million of Hinge’s 24.6 million covered lives as of the end of 2025. This means they have essentially no penetration into 1) smaller employers, and 2) large national health plans or Medicare.

Focusing on the opportunity in non-self-insured customers, management reported that non-ASO (non-Administrative Services Only; essentially non-self-insured) grew 100% in 2025 (off a very small base). Currently, Hinge has less than 3 million covered lives across non-ASO markets, with the total opportunity across fully-insured, Medicare Advantage, and Federal Insurance totaling up to 96 million lives.

This feels like a real tangible opportunity—management acknowledged on the call that these customers are “going to be an important part of our future growth,” while also adding that these customers are even more discerning and exacting when it comes to demonstrable cost savings, which Hinge continues to deliver great results on, and which management believes will continue to convert into covered lives expansion over time.

Enso TENS Device

Acquired in 2021, Enso is a TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation) device that has been proven to decrease pain and improve physical therapy outcomes. Enso has a number of studies that have been published (including this one in back pain patients and this one for back and knee pain), including one that the company uses to claim that Enso is 2.3x more effective at pain improvement versus other TENS devices.

Hinge management estimated on the 3Q25 call that ~25% of patients would use Enso during 2025 (up from 15% in 2024 and 5% in 2023); management also shared that patients receiving Enso complete ~70% more exercise therapy sessions (theoretically because the pain reduction makes it easier to do exercises). The integration of Enso adds to the effectiveness and the engagement of Hinge’s platform. For what it is worth, an expert call I listened to with a benefits manager at a large labor-intensive employer cited Enso as one of the key reasons they contracted with Hinge.

It also seems possible that Hinge may try to add additional hardware products over time, though it is hard to think of a device that would be as well suited to Hinge’s platform as a TENS device.

HingeSelect

Launched in June 2025, HingeSelect is basically Hinge’s foray into a telemedicine network—it aims to create a network of orthopedic and other healthcare professionals that patients can connect with for remote consultations, with the ultimate goal of avoiding unnecessary invasive procedures. Depending on the best path for the patient, HingeSelect also connects patients with imaging, physical therapy, or procedure facilities that are part of the HingeSelect network (are vetted, have reviews, etc.). The goal is to create a two-sided marketplace where MSK patients can seek consultations and find care providers.

As of 3Q25, HingeSelect had added over 3,300 provider locations across all 50 states. Management plans to continue investing in building out the network in 2026 and guides to meaningful (though still small on a relative basis) revenue generation in 2027.

AI Integration

In October, Hinge announced the rollout of two AI-powered tools: Movement Analysis, providing more detailed movement and progress tracking over time using Hinge’s computer vision system, and Robin, an always-on AI care/support assistant.

Management has shared some encouraging earlier data points, particularly from Robin, including:

  • 28% reduction in average time spent by the care team on asynchronous support sessions from Q3 to Q4.

  • 92% positive rating from patients that engaged with Robin.

Hinge is still adding features to Robin, including the ability to answer common patient questions.

While neither product is completely groundbreaking, these types of incremental improvements, in addition to Hinge’s other/internal AI integration initiatives, which allowed the company’s support team expense to remain flat in 2025, contribute to enhancing both patient experience and cost efficiency.

R&D and Potential Acquisitions

As I have touched on, Hinge’s ~$500 million cash position and the fact that it will generate something like $250 million of free cash flow in 2026 mean that it is free to invest in R&D and to entertain acquisitions, whether a tuck-in, a new therapy device, or a rising/complementary competitor.

SaaSpocalypse Defensibility

In addition to strong current performance and solid long-term and supplementary growth vectors, I also believe that Hinge is more defensible against the prevailing AI SaaSpocalypse narrative than most software verticals.

Topically, the first question in the Q&A was regarding Hinge’s differentiation and defensiveness versus the threat of AI (in particular, AI’s ability to allow for much cheaper and better software). The CEO had a nicely prepared response that included four key pillars:

  1. Barriers to entry in healthcare: CEO Daniel Perez points out that Hinge has spent a decade building relationships with health plans, PBMs, and employers, relationships that required extensive work not only on Hinge’s end but also on the part of the client/customer/partner (work they would be unlikely to do over again, especially as Hinge continues to deliver successful outcomes and satisfaction). Hinge is also partnered with all five of the major health plans/PBMs, which makes it extremely easy for clients/customers to add Hinge to their coverage—I listened to one expert call where the expert called it the “easy button” in terms of the onboarding process. In general, the complexity and bureaucracy and just the nature of the healthcare/healthcare benefits industry present higher barriers to new entrants versus most other software verticals.
  3. Proprietary data: Perez also cites that they have data from ~100 million treatment sessions, which has allowed them to continually improve their product and which an AI or any new software entrant would not have access to. This is a fine reason, but not the most compelling in my view, though I do think it touches on the fact that Hinge’s platform, the product itself, and the care it provides are simply great, and that that is hard to recreate, whether using AI or not.
  4. Hardware integration and in-person care: Perez emphasizes the fact that the Hinge now integrates hardware (e.g., the Enso TENS unit) and in-person care (through the fledgling HingeSelect product), both of which, and especially the in-person integration, increase Hinge’s moat. Like I said, in an expert call, a benefits manager at a large labor-intensive employer cited Enso as one of the key reasons they contracted with Hinge.
  5. Long-term outcomes studies: Hinge has an impressive body of published research validating the effectiveness of its platform for various conditions, including those extending 2+ years. This is quite a strong selling point for clients/customers and is obviously impossible to replicate in the short term.

Adding my own ideas about Hinge’s AI defensibility, I would say:

  1. There is a dynamic/phenomenon in a vertical like digital physical therapy where once you are quite clearly ahead of the competition (e.g., market share leader, best-of-breed product, most clinical/outcomes/ROI data), and especially when customer satisfaction is so high, there is 1) no incentive for existing customers to switch providers or drop coverage, and 2) the choice for new potential customers becomes almost obvious (almost a network/reputation effect).

  2. In a similar vein, when you are cash flow rich like Hinge (and are sitting on an already substantial pile of cash), with the right direction from leadership, it is comparatively easier for you to maintain leadership through continual R&D investment and potential acquisitions than it is for a new entrant to gain market share.

  3. Lastly, if you really think about AI software disruption in terms of the vulnerability of the end market that a particular software/SaaS product sells into, I believe you come to the conclusion that Hinge’s digital physical therapy market vertical is comparatively quite safe. Take the sales profession that a software like Salesforce targets (SDRs, BDRs)—there is an argument to be made that AI (and AI agents) will be able to do 90%+ of the work that an SDR/BDR human does in the relatively near future (e.g., emailing, scheduling, pursuing leads, educating on the product), which, if it came to fruition, would result in massive reductions in employment numbers, potentially vastly reducing the number of customers/seats that a company like Salesforce can sell into. That dynamic simply doesn’t exist with Hinge and its vertical—people will continue to have (perhaps an increasing amount of) MSK problems that require regimens to address. (I suppose you could argue that if AI really displaces whole industries, the resultant drop in employment would also reduce Hinge’s enterprise customer opportunity, but we can deal with that when we get to it).

While I think these factors make Hinge relatively insulated, at least much more so than many software/SaaS companies, the AI disruption conversation does seem to increase the potential risk of a cheap, high-quality virtual physical therapy app that is widely-available direct-to-consumer through Apple/Android app stores (i.e., without going through an employer).It is true that, to a large extent, these apps already exist or have been tried, but advancements in and ubiquitization of both AI and computer vision should make it even easier. That said, many of the competitive advantages mentioned above (as well as acquisitive power) should provide protection.

As the tide/panic of the AI apocalypse narrative goes out, I expect Hinge to gradually become recognized as having a solid AI moat.

(Bonus thought: I am wondering if, once the market eventually feels that it is actually getting a handle on and some clarity on which software companies/verticals are going to struggle versus be fine in the AI era, if the companies that are deemed (or prove to be) immune and/or beneficiaries may actually command a premium valuation relative to where they would’ve traded pre-SaaSpocalypse because of the increased scarcity value of high gross margin, defensible software/SaaS names.)

Competition

The competitive landscape in the digital MSK therapy space is relatively sparse, with Sword Health the clear top competitor and not much else of significance beyond that

Sword Health (and recent Kaia Health acquisition)

Sword Health is a provider of MSK-focused digital health solutions, which it sells to employers and health plans, so it is quite comparable to Hinge. It offers personalized physical therapy for joint and back pain, women’s pelvic health (e.g., pelvic pain, urinary incontinence), and whole-body movement and injury prevention. Sword is private and raised $40 million at a reported $4 billion valuation last June (with the big names like General Catalyst and Khosla leading the deal)—at the same time, it was reported that Sword was at a $240 million annual revenue run rate (i.e., less than half of Hinge’s $588 million 2025 revenue). The Sword Health app has 4.7 stars with 40k reviews on the Apple App Store (vs. 4.9 stars on 122k reviews for Hinge, to get a sense of size/scale).

Last month it was announced that Sword was acquiring pulmonary and MSK care-focused Kaia Health for $285 million. The Kaia Health app has 4.7 stars with (a measly) 5.5k reviews on the Apple App Store.

CEO Daniel Perez stated on the 4Q25 earnings call that Hinge’s revenue remains multiples larger than the Sword and Kaia combined entity—based on the $240 million revenue run rate reported for Sword last June and something like $40 million of revenue for Kaia (based on news stories and acquisition price), Hinge is more like twice the size of the combined competitor.

Perez also added that Hinge has seen “many customers from both of them switch over to Hinge Health over the past 12 months.” Given Hinge’s retention metrics, it seems likely that there is minimal switching in the opposite direction, which seems to further speak to Hinge’s status as the best-in-class operator in the space.

The CEO also added that they briefly looked at Kaia as an acquisition target before passing. He then sums up how he sees the competitive landscape quite well:

Omada Health

Omada is mostly focused on between-visit care for diabetes, hypertension, and other metabolic-associated conditions, but also has a smaller MSK offering. Omada is still pretty small, with a $270 million revenue run rate in 3Q25. While Omada doesn’t break out its segments, its clear focus is on metabolic diseases, with MSK maybe making up something like 20% of the business (likely lower), meaning Hinge is likely at least 10x as large as Omada’s MSK offering on a revenue basis. Omada’s Apple app has 4.7 stars with 93k reviews.

MedBridge Go

MedBridge Go isn’t directly comparable to Hinge, as it is a product for physical therapists and healthcare professionals to design between-visit exercise plans for patients—I thought it was adjacent enough that there could be some overlap. It has 4.8 stars with 120k reviews.

Beyond these three, there don’t appear to be any other significant digital physical therapy apps that I can find, with smaller operators/apps with varying degrees of MSK exposure such as Dario Health, Vori Health, Prehab, and Limber, as well as things like yoga apps that may have some demographic overlap with Hinge.

Financial Model

Base Case

Below is my base case for Hinge, with 2026 revenue growth of 25%, exactly in line with management’s guidance, and zero FCF margin expansion (slight EBIT margin expansion but offset by higher capex).

Bull Case

My upside scenario projects 32% revenue growth in 2026, which assumes an increase in yield to ~4.2% (smaller than the jump in 2025); this scenario also assumes a slower growth deceleration in 2027 (24%) and 2028 (19%). I also assume that 2026 growth will drive a modest continuation of the dramatic operating leverage and FCF expansion that was seen in 2025, with an FCF margin expanding to 34%.

Valuation

As of Friday’s close, using my Base Case projections, Hinge is trading at 4.6x 2026 sales, 18.6x EBIT (treating non-GAAP EBIT as equivalent to EBITDA for the sake of comparison), and 14.6x 2026 FCF.

Unfortunately, as you can see below, there aren’t a ton of great comps for Hinge. I discuss some thoughts on the comps and valuation below.

Fundamentals

(Data from CapIQ. Forward projections using sell-side consensus.)

Valuation

Omada is clearly the closest comparable company, yet it is smaller, resides in a much more competitive metabolic disease segment, and is still significantly unprofitable. Talkspace is very minimally profitable, has structurally lower margins, and also operates in a much more crowded virtual mental health services market. Him & Hers is a completely different business/model from Hinge and is currently in the midst of having their GLP-1 compounding business, their primary growth driver, erased by the FDA.

While most molecular diagnostics companies like BillionToOne, Natera, Tempus AI, and Caris Life Sciences are unprofitable or minimally profitable, I took a look at them in the absence of good comps. It is interesting to note Caris Life Sciences, which is a similar size and has similar growth projections to Hinge, yet is quite unprofitable but is in the midst of turning a profit, is trading at multiples ~30% higher than Hinge (looking primarily at 2027 to give Caris’ margins time to mature), despite what I would argue are worse fundamentals and major competitive concerns (e.g., BillionToOne, Tempus, etc.).

Lastly, I thought it would be interesting to look at general/corporate software names like Salesforce and Monday.com. Both of which, to me at least, feel like exactly the type of software that AI is threatening to disrupt/replace, with Monday.com feeling the much more readily vulnerable of the two just because of Salesforce’s deep integration with customers. Looking at FCF, Hinge (14.6x) is basically valued in line with Monday.com (13.5x) at current levels, despite higher growth, higher margins, and a much stronger and safer fundamental outlook.

And then keep in mind that in my Bull Case, which I think is likely to be closer to reality than the Base Case that management has guided to, Hinge is currently trading at 12.6x 2026 FCF.

Hinge has 40-50% upside if it were to trade back towards its all-time highs reached in Summer 2025, which would still represent a discount to its all-time high trading multiples.

As I said above, I expect that the market will start to be able to more accurately differentiate between software/SaaS winners and losers over the next couple of quarters, with defensible companies like Hinge likely re-rating or simply returning to higher multiples.

Risks

Free/Low-Cost Alternative: Irrespective of the impact of AI on software development, it seems to me that there is the potential for a direct-to-consumer app or platform that provides most of the same physical therapy-like services as Hinge but is available for anyone to download on Apple/Android and is relatively cheap.

Competition and Margin Compression: Much more likely than outright displacement or disruption by AI-enabled software generation, it seems much more likely that competitors, small and large (maybe even a health plan), could eventually come up with a similarly-effective digital physical therapy platform that slowly ate into Hinge’s business and TAM, potentially also resulting in pricing pressure and lower margins.

GLP-1s: Another expert call with a Hinge customer that I listened to, while saying the Hinge platform was excellent and that they expect the percentage of employees using the platform to continue increasing, also mentioned a massive increase in the number of their employees taking GLP-1s over the last year, which could absolutely reduce MSK condition incidences.

Large Enterprise Expansion: Hinge currently serves 53% of the Fortune 100 and 45% of the Fortune 500. While there is still a lot of solid runway with America’s largest employers, there is also the simple reality that Hinge has certainly already made contact with most, if not all, of these remaining potential large enterprises who, up until now, have not converted. Adding additional customers gets harder over time.

Growth Deceleration: The company’s 783k members in 2025 was a 47% increase versus 2024, driven by both newly-added covered lives and increased yield. Growth deceleration is inevitable, and while my estimates for 2027 and 2028 feel quite reasonable, I could be wrong.

Yield: It is hard to get a handle on just how much the company should/will be able to continue increasing the yield of its covered lives actually engaging/using Hinge, which was a major contributor to growth in 2025. While it has consistently grown since 2023, continued expansion relies on marketing campaigns, product-led growth campaigns, and other factors/initiatives that are hard to predict.

Valuations: It is possible that the valuation discount being placed on software/SaaS names that has taken place over the last month, and really over the last 6 months, becomes kind of the new normal, where software as a whole becomes so unpredictable in an AI era that investors never feel quite as confident as they once did.

Self-Cannibalism: The idea that the better the MSK care provided by Hinge’s platform, the quicker the resolution of the patient’s symptoms, and thus the shorter the duration of engagement for each patient. This is not a super serious concern.

Conclusion

Perhaps it is because Hinge has only been publicly traded for ~8 months, or because it is small, or because there are no directly comparable publicly-traded comps—for whatever reason, Hinge is not being given credit for essentially superb underlying growth and profitability, being a clear market leader, and having a clear sight line to continued growth in coming years. Additionally, Hinge appears to be far more defensible than most software companies to the disruptive potential of AI, which, as it is realized over the next couple/few quarters, should drive a re-rating back towards previous trading multiples.

Hinge is a strong business trading very cheaply, anywhere between 12-14x 2026 FCF depending on your projections, which should trade back towards its all-time highs (~40-50% upside) as the company continues to report quarters of operational and cash flow-generating excellence.

data.title: Touchstone Flexible Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Highlights

  • Seeks a high level of income consistent with reasonable risk by investing primarily in income producing securities
  • Primarily invests in investment grade corporate bonds, high yield corporate bonds, preferred stocks, U.S. municipal bonds and U.S. Treasuries
  • Actively manages the portfolio by rotating among asset classes and tactically hedging during various interest rate and market environments
  • Seeks to identify relative value across asset classes and capture opportunities primarily within the corporate, U.S. Treasury, municipal and preferred security markets
  • Analyzes and targets the portfolio's level of risk and interest rate sensitivity
  • Selects individual positions based on security credit metrics and structures
  • Focuses on liquid securities with transparent pricing and actively-traded capital structures

Market Recap

U.S. equity and fixed income markets posted positive returns in the fourth quarter of 2025. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates by 25 basis points in both October and December, lowering the upper bound of the federal funds rate from 4.25% to 3.75%. These cuts were driven by continued softening in labor market data, while some dissent remained due to concerns about persistent inflation.

Economic growth strengthened during the quarter, with annualized U.S. GDP growth rising from 3.8% to 4.3%, while core personal consumption inflation eased slightly to 2.8%, remaining above the Fed's 2% target. The Federal Open Market Committee reiterated its data-dependent approach to policy. Markets are pricing in pauses at early 2026 meetings, with expectations for additional rate cuts later in the year, broadly consistent with the December dot plot projecting a 3.38% policy rate for 2026.

Fixed income markets were modestly positive, with the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index gaining 1.1% for the quarter. The yield curve steepened as the 10-year Treasury yield edged higher to 4.17%, while both the 2's–10's spread (the difference between the yield of the 10-year U.S.

Treasury Note and the 2-year U.S. Treasury Note) and 5's–30's spread (the difference between the yield of the 30-year U.S. Treasury Bond and the 5-year U.S. Treasury Note) widened. Equity markets also advanced, with the S&P 500 rising 2.66% quarter-over-quarter.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Flexible Income Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

Underperformance was primarily driven by the Fund's shorter duration and underweight Treasury allocation relative to the benchmark. The Fund's portfolio continues to emphasize high current income, relatively short duration (generally under five years), and a strong margin of safety through rigorous fundamental and credit analysis. At quarter end 2025, the Fund was diversified across investment-grade (IG) and high-yield (HY) corporates, preferred securities (PFD), seasoned prime jumbo residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), agency commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), and prime auto asset-backed securities (ABS). PFD holdings in fixed-to-reset structures outperformed and remain favored over long-duration fixed-rate securities due to tighter spreads and greater interest rate sensitivity. These structures benefit from higher reset coupons, reduced volatility, and the potential for shorter duration as call incentives increase if rates remain elevated.

The Fund continued to add to seasoned RMBS, which offer attractive total return potential supported by structural deleveraging, strong credit enhancement, and resilient housing fundamentals. Select long duration IG credits issued during the low-rate COVID period were also added at discounted prices, offering compelling risk-adjusted returns given low dollar prices, positive convexity, and yields above historical averages. Agency CMBS holdings backed by multi-family properties provide high-quality exposure with low effective duration and strong credit metrics. While corporate and securitized credit spreads remain tight relative to long-term averages, yields are elevated due to higher Treasury rates, with securitized spreads still wider than corporates. Given this backdrop, the Fund remained cautious toward lower-quality and longer-duration credit, favoring shorter, higher-quality investments and selectively building cash where appropriate.

Fund positioning evolved modestly during the quarter, reflecting relative value, income generation, and risk management considerations. The Fund reduced its PFD allocation following strong performance, expected calls, and positions reaching target prices. Remaining holdings emphasize securities trading to near-term call dates (2025–2026) with attractive back-end resets, while select higher-coupon PFD were added where yields were viewed as sufficient compensation for extension risk. IG corporate exposure also declined, though the Fund maintained conviction in junior subordinated utility credits and discounted long-duration IG bonds issued during the COVID-era low-rate environment. These securities, purchased opportunistically during periods of spread widening or rising long-end yields, continue to offer compelling risk-adjusted return potential due to low dollar prices and strong credit quality.

Within IG structured products, the Fund maintained exposure to high-quality, higher-carry assets such as credit risk transfer securities backed by seasoned RMBS and prime auto loans, which offer shorter duration and higher yields relative to IG corporates. A barbell duration approach remained in place, balancing elevated front-end yields with select longer-duration discounted IG RMBS that benefit from positive convexity and potential calls or faster prepayments. While credit spreads have tightened meaningfully since entering these positions in 2023, the Fund remains opportunistic. Exposure to collateralized loan obligations and single-family rental mortgage-backed securities was reduced in favor of more attractive risk-adjusted opportunities elsewhere.

HY exposure increased, driven by additions to high-quality corporate credits with strong liquidity, large market capitalizations, and no CCC-rated exposure. The focus remains on issuers with durable fundamentals and shorter spread duration to limit downside risk in a tight-spread environment. In contrast, HY structured products exposure declined modestly, though allocations to agency-backed multi-family CMBS, seasoned RMBS, and prime auto ABS were maintained or selectively increased. These securitized assets continue to offer attractive total return potential supported by strong structural credit enhancement, resilient borrower performance, and relatively short, front-loaded cash flows.

Municipal bond exposure increased, though duration views are primarily expressed through U.S. Treasuries given relative value considerations and ongoing fiscal pressures in select states. Treasury exposure, concentrated in 30-year bonds, was modestly increased as the Fund extended duration opportunistically at higher long-end yields amid expectations for softer labor data and eventual rate cuts. Cash levels also rose, reflecting a patient approach and positioning for future opportunities. At quarter-end, the Fund's duration was approximately 5.9 years versus 6.0 years for the benchmark, with 65% of duration six years or less. This shorter-duration posture helped mitigate volatility and benefited relative performance during the quarter's bull steepening, as front-end rates rallied while long-term yields edged higher.

Outlook and Conclusion

The Fund's defensive positioning—characterized by lower duration and elevated cash—remains both a source of protection and a potential near-term headwind. While this posture helps mitigate risks from inflation, rising Treasury yields, and credit deterioration, it could modestly lag peers if longer-dated Treasury yields decline sharply. Although nominal yields across credit markets are more attractive than in recent years, credit spreads remain historically tight, prompting continued caution toward more credit-sensitive assets such as generic HY. Exposure to these areas will remain selective and tactical, given their higher default risk.

Looking ahead, we expect elevated volatility in 2026 as labor market conditions weaken and economic fundamentals potentially deteriorate more than currently anticipated by the Fed. With markets pricing in multiple policy rate cuts, declining yields could support faster prepayments and higher total returns in assets with positive convexity, particularly discounted, prime-oriented mortgage sectors. The Fund is positioned to perform across both “higher-for-longer” and recessionary scenarios, supported by high starting yields and ample liquidity. We expect active management and tactical deployment of cash and shorter-duration investments to be key drivers of risk management and return generation as conditions evolve.
